title: MLPS / PERCEPTRONS
sub: Visual exam summary — every section maps to a real quiz question.
source: Source · Tilburg — Introduction to Deep Learning · Lecture 1 (Multilayer Perceptrons)

sections.title: THE PERCEPTRON
sections.exam: Computation: take the SUM of products of inputs and weights, add the bias, pass through the STEP activation -> binary 0/1; in o = f(w0 + Σ wᵢxᵢ), f is the activation function.
sections.ascii:
inputs        weights
  x1 ──w1─┐
  x2 ──w2─┤
  x3 ──w3─┼─►( Σ )─► z ─►[ f ]─► o ∈ {0,1}
   ⋮      │     ▲         step
 +1 ──w0──┘    bias       f = activation function

 z = w0 + Σ wᵢ·xᵢ        o = 1 if z ≥ 0 else 0

figures.label: WEB DIAGRAM
figures.desc: Inputs xᵢ scaled by weights wᵢ, summed (Σ = the transfer function = 'sum of products'), then squashed by the activation φ. The Σ box is exactly blank #1; the step box is f.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: The lecturer's perceptron: x→w→Σ→f→output o, compared to expected y to get an error. Output is a binary 0/1 because f is the step function.

sections.title: LINEAR THRESHOLD & DESIGN
sections.exam: A perceptron is ONE linear threshold: it fires (o=1) iff w·x + b ≥ 0 — so pick weights/bias to set that boundary; a zero weight-column makes that input irrelevant and the step counts z=0 as 1.
sections.ascii:
fire o=1  ⇔  w·x + b ≥ 0      (step: H(0)=1, so z=0 → 1)

AND (separable, 1 line):     design a threshold:
 x1 x2 │ y                     all wᵢ=0.02, b=−1
  0  0 │ 0                     Σ = 0.02·(#ones) − 1
  1  0 │ 0                     crosses 0 near ~50 ones (mc5=A)
  0  1 │ 0
  1  1 │ 1     zero W-column ⇒ that xᵢ is IGNORED (exam Q15/Q30)

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: A single perceptron can only draw a straight decision line, splitting the plane into two half-spaces — the equation o = f(w0 + Σ wᵢxᵢ) printed beside it.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: AND's four points: the lone (1,1)=1 can be cut off from the three 0's by one line, so one perceptron handles it. This is the kind of weight/bias design the quiz tests.

sections.title: THE UPDATE RULE
sections.exam: Perceptron learning: error = target − output; Δwᵢ = η·(y−o)·xᵢ so d_weights = lr*error*inputs and d_bias = lr*error; then weights = weights + d_weights and bias += d_bias.
sections.ascii:
Δwᵢ = η·(y − o)·xᵢ        wᵢ(t+1) = wᵢ(t) + Δwᵢ

 error     = target − output      # (y−o)  NOT weights*inputs
 d_weights = lr * error * inputs   # η·(y−o)·xᵢ
 d_bias    = lr * error            # bias has input = 1
 weights   = weights + d_weights    # ADD the delta (move toward target)
 bias     += d_bias

 o == y → error 0 → no change.   wrong → nudge each w along its xᵢ.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Output o is compared with expected y to form the error; that error 'updates the parameters' — the closed loop the update-rule code implements line by line.

sections.title: XOR — WHY ONE LAYER FAILS
sections.exam: A single perceptron draws only ONE line, but XOR is NOT linearly separable, so it needs a hidden layer; a 2-layer sigmoid MLP with the right weights solves XOR.
sections.ascii:
XOR: 1 when inputs DIFFER — no single line splits these 4 points:

 x2
  1 │ (0,1)=1        (1,1)=0     the two 1's sit on a
    │                            DIAGONAL from the two 0's
  0 │ (0,0)=0        (1,0)=1     → one straight line can't
    └──────────────── x1            separate them
        0       1

 single layer → ✗      ≥1 hidden layer (2 lines) → ✓   (mc13 = not separable)

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: XOR's truth table (0/1/1/0) and its four points: the two 1's are diagonal to the two 0's, so no straight line separates them — the definition of 'not linearly separable'.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Stacking two perceptrons draws two lines; points BETWEEN the lines become one class and the rest the other — the hidden layer that makes XOR solvable.

figures.label: WEB DIAGRAM
figures.desc: A worked 2-layer net for XOR: a hidden layer of threshold units feeding one output. Mirrors mc12, where only the correctly-signed weight matrices reproduce XOR.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: After the hidden layer the four points are re-mapped so a single line now separates them — visual proof that depth fixes XOR.

sections.title: WHY MLPS APPROXIMATE NON-LINEAR FUNCTIONS
sections.exam: Non-linear activation functions BETWEEN layers let the composition of multiple linear transformations represent non-linear input→output mappings (mc14=A).
sections.ascii:
linear-only stack COLLAPSES:
   W2·(W1·x) = (W2·W1)·x = W'·x   → still ONE line, no gain

add a non-linear f between layers:
   a1 = f(W1·x + b1)     ← non-linear bend
   o  = f(W2·a1 + b2)    → curved decision boundary

 NOT depth alone, NOT bias, NOT memorising — the NON-LINEARITY does it.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: A layer = several perceptrons sharing the same inputs, each with its own weights/bias and activation f. Stacking these layers builds the MLP.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Input, hidden and output layers fully connected (dense). The hidden layer plus its non-linear f is what lets the network bend its decision surface.

sections.title: MLP AS LINEAR ALGEBRA
sections.exam: Weights are MATRICES connecting layers, biases are VECTORS, and a layer's output is computed by matrix multiplication plus addition: o = f(Wx + b) (mc11=B).
sections.ascii:
ONE LAYER = MATRIX MULTIPLY + ADD     o = f( W x + b )

   W (out×in)        x (in)      b (out)
   ┌ w11 w12 w13 ┐   ┌ x1 ┐      ┌ b1 ┐
   │ w21 w22 w23 │ · │ x2 │  +   │ b2 │  → f(·) → o
   └  …          ┘   └ x3 ┘      └ …  ┘

 deep:  a⁰ = x ,  aˡ = f(Wˡ aˡ⁻¹ + bˡ) ,  o = aⁿ

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: The per-neuron sum oⱼ = f(bⱼ + Σ wⱼᵢxᵢ) rewritten as one compact matrix-vector equation o = f(Wx + b) — weights become a matrix W, biases a vector b.

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: Every layer: aˡ = f(Wˡ aˡ⁻¹ + bˡ), with a⁰ = x and the network output o = aⁿ. This is the exact notation the backprop chain-rule questions use.

sections.title: TRAINING LOOP & BACKPROP MATH
sections.exam: Four steps: forward (feed input in), error (compute the cost), backward (gradient of cost w.r.t. each W,b via the chain rule), update (move params OPPOSITE the gradient).
sections.ascii:
1 FORWARD : feed inputs        a⁰=x → … → o
2 ERROR   : compute cost J(o,y)
3 BACKWARD: ∂J/∂Wˡ via CHAIN RULE
            δˡ = (∂J/∂zˡ⁺¹)·(∂zˡ⁺¹/∂aˡ)·(∂aˡ/∂zˡ)
4 UPDATE  : W ← W − η·∂J/∂W      (step OPPOSITE the gradient)

 chain pieces (sigmoid):  ∂zˡ⁺¹/∂aˡ = Wˡ⁺¹   ∂J/∂zˡ⁺¹ = δˡ⁺¹
                          ∂aˡ/∂zˡ   = σ(zˡ)(1−σ(zˡ))

 MEMORY/iter = (#params incl. biases) × factor   [SGD ×2 · momentum ×3 · Adam ×4]
 params/layer = in·out + out      (the +out = biases)

figures.label: LECTURE SLIDE
figures.desc: The loss L (a.k.a. error or cost) measures the distance between the network output and the expected y — exactly the 'error calculation' step that backprop differentiates.

head: Step
What happens
Match
rows: input data is fed into the network
the cost function output is computed
gradient of cost w.r.t. each W,b via the chain rule
params modified OPPOSITE the gradient direction
